content:
A light ray of wavelength 546 nm is incident on an air-glass interface. If the speed of light in air is $3 \times 10^8 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$. Then, wavelength of light ray in glass will be

[Take, refractive index of glass, $\mu=1.5$ ]
explanation:
The problem involves determining the wavelength of a light ray as it enters a glass medium from the air. Initially, the wavelength of the light ray is 546 nm in air. The speed of light in air is $3 \times 10^8 \, \text{m/s}$, and the refractive index of glass is given as $\mu = 1.5$.

To find the wavelength of light in glass, we use the relation between the refractive index and wavelength:

$ \mu = \frac{\text{Wavelength in air} (\lambda)}{\text{Wavelength in glass} (\lambda_g)} $

Rearranging the formula to solve for the wavelength in glass:

$ \lambda_g = \frac{\lambda}{\mu} $

Substitute the given values:

$ \lambda_g = \frac{546 \, \text{nm}}{1.5} $

Calculating this gives:

$ \lambda_g = 364 \, \text{nm} $

Thus, the wavelength of the light ray in glass is 364 nm.

content: The focal length of thin convex lens for red and blue rays are $$100 \mathrm{~cm}$$ and $$98.01 \mathrm{~cm}$$, respectively. There, the dispersive power of the material of the lens is
explanation:
Given, $$f_r=100 \mathrm{~cm}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & f_m=\sqrt{f_r \times f_s}=\sqrt{98.01 \times 100}=99 \mathrm{~cm} \\ & f_b=98.01 \mathrm{~cm} \end{aligned}$$

We have, $$f_r-f_b=\omega f_m$$

$$\omega=\frac{f_t-f_b}{f_m}$$

Dispersive power.

$$\omega=\frac{100-98.01}{99}=0.020 \Rightarrow \omega=0.020$$

content: If an object of height $$6 \mathrm{~cm}$$ is viewed through a liquid of refractive index $$\frac{4}{3}$$ vertically at a depth of $$10 \mathrm{~cm}$$. Then what will be its apparent height and apparent distance from the surface of liquid.
explanation:
Given, $$n=\frac{4}{3}$$, height of object, $$y=6 \mathrm{~cm}$$, depth of liquid, $$x=10 \mathrm{~cm}$$

$$\therefore$$ Apparent depth or height of object from liquid surface $$=n y=\frac{4}{3} \times 6=8 \mathrm{~cm}$$

Apparent distance $$=x+n y=10+8=18 \mathrm{~cm}$$

content: A plane glass mirror of thickness $$3 \mathrm{~cm}$$ of material of $$\mu=\frac{3}{2}$$ is silvered on the back surface. When a point object is placed $$9 \mathrm{~cm}$$ from the front surface of the mirror, then the position of the brightest image from the front surface is
explanation:
A thick glass mirror produces a number of images. There is an apparent shift of actual silvered surface towards the unsilvered face

Effective distance of the reflecting surface from unsilvered face $$=\frac{d}{\mu}=\frac{3}{3 / 2} \mathrm{~cm}=2 \mathrm{~cm}$$

Distance of point object from effective reflecting surface $$=9 \mathrm{~cm}+2 \mathrm{~cm}=11 \mathrm{~cm}$$

Distance of image from point object

$$=11 \mathrm{~cm}+11 \mathrm{~cm}=22 \mathrm{~cm}$$

Distance of image from unsilvered face

$$=(22-9) \mathrm{cm}=13 \mathrm{~cm}$$

content: An object is $$8 \mathrm{~cm}$$ high. It is desired to form a real image $$4 \mathrm{~cm}$$ high at $$60 \mathrm{~cm}$$ from the mirror. The type of mirror needed with the focal length is
